conversation.content:
Reconcile the following columns. Document has 14 pages. Sources are anonymous (A and B).

COLUMNS:

---
Column 1: Treatment Class
Definition: What is the categorization or classification of the treatment or therapy received by participants in the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="androgen axis inhibitor" | page=2 | modality=text | reasoning="The abstract and context sections describe orteronel as a 'nonsteroidal 17,20-lyase inhibitor suppressing androgen synthesis' and a 'novel androgen axis inhibitor'."
  B: value="CYP17 inhibitor" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 1 states 'Orteronel (TAK-700) is a nonsteroidal 17,20-lyase inhibitor suppressing androgen synthesis.' and 'Orteronel (TAK-700) is a novel CYP17 inhibitor.'"

---
Column 2: Trial
Definition: What name of the clinical study or experiment being referenced?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="SWOG-1216" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The trial name 'SWOG-1216' is explicitly stated in the title and methods section."
  B: value="SWOG-1216" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 1 clearly states the trial name in the title."

---
Column 3: Type of Therapy
Definition: What is the description of the type of treatment or intervention provided to participants in the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="ADT with orteronel (experimental arm); ADT with bicalutamide (control arm)" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The methods section describes the randomization arms: 'ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily; experimental arm) or ADT with bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily; control arm).'"
  B: value="Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) with Orteronel or Bicalutamide" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 1 describes the experimental arm as 'ADT with orteronel' and the control arm as 'ADT with bicalutamide'."

---
Column 4: Docetaxel administration - N (%) | Treatment
Definition: What percentage of participants in the treatment arm of the clinical trial who received docetaxel before or during the trial? If reported, include count and/or percentage. If multiple relevant arms are reported, list each arm separately. Use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document states that patients were enrolled if they were 'unsuitable candidates for docetaxel or if they declined docetaxel therapy', indicating docetaxel was not part of the initial protocol treatment. Docetaxel is mentioned as a subsequent life-prolonging therapy, meaning after discontinuation"
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="Docetaxel is mentioned as a subsequent therapy, not part of the protocol treatment for the treatment arm. Page 2 mentions patients being unsuitable for or declining docetaxel, and page 6 mentions it as a subsequent life-prolonging therapy. Table A2 on page 12 also lists 'STAMPEDE docetaxel' as a sep"

---
Column 5: Docetaxel administration - N (%) | Control
Definition: What number and percentage of participants who received docetaxel before or during the trial in the control arm of the clinical trial? If reported, include count and/or percentage. If multiple relevant arms are reported, list each arm separately. Use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document states that patients were enrolled if they were 'unsuitable candidates for docetaxel or if they declined docetaxel therapy', indicating docetaxel was not part of the initial protocol treatment. Docetaxel is mentioned as a subsequent life-prolonging therapy, meaning after discontinuation"
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="Docetaxel is mentioned as a subsequent therapy, not part of the protocol treatment for the control arm. Page 2 mentions patients being unsuitable for or declining docetaxel, and page 6 mentions it as a subsequent life-prolonging therapy. Table A2 on page 12 also lists 'STAMPEDE docetaxel' as a separ"

---
Column 6: Median Age (years) | Treatment
Definition: What median age in years of participants included in the treatment arm of the clinical trial? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="67.6" | page=5 | modality=table | reasoning="Table 1, 'Median age, years (range)' for 'Orteronel (n = 638)' arm is '67.6 (46.1-90.0)'."
  B: value="67.6 years" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 4 states: 'The median age of the patients for the orteronel and control arms was 67.6 and 68.1 years, respectively.' Orteronel arm is the treatment arm."

---
Column 7: Median Age (years) | Control
Definition: What the median age of participants included in the control arm of the clinical trial? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="68.1" | page=5 | modality=table | reasoning="Table 1, 'Median age, years (range)' for 'Bicalutamide (n = 641)' arm is '68.1 (19.4-92.3)'."
  B: value="68.1 years" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 4 states: 'The median age of the patients for the orteronel and control arms was 67.6 and 68.1 years, respectively.' Control arm is the bicalutamide arm."

---
Column 8: Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Treatment
Definition: What is the median duration in months that participants in the treatment arm of the trial received on-treatment therapy? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document reports median PFS and median follow-up for the study, but not a specific 'median on-treatment duration' for each arm."
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="This specific metric was not found in the provided pages. While page 13, Figure A1, shows patient status (on/off treatment), it does not provide the median duration."

---
Column 9: Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Control
Definition: What is the median duration in months of participants receiving treatment in the control arm of the clinical trial? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document reports median PFS and median follow-up for the study, but not a specific 'median on-treatment duration' for each arm."
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="This specific metric was not found in the provided pages. While page 13, Figure A1, shows patient status (on/off treatment), it does not provide the median duration."

---
Column 10: No. of Deaths - N | Treatment
Definition: What the number of participants who died in the treatment arm of the clinical trial?.
  A: value="251" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="The 'Primary End Point' section states '251 in the orteronel group' and Figure 1, 'Deaths' column for 'LHRHa plus orteronel' is '251'."
  B: value="251" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 4 states: 'Analysis for OS occurred after the prespecified 525 deaths were observed (251 in the orteronel group and 274 in the control group).' Orteronel group is the treatment arm."

---
Column 11: No. of Deaths - N | Control
Definition: What number of deaths that occurred in the control arm of the clinical trial?.
  A: value="274" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="The 'Primary End Point' section states '274 in the control group' and Figure 1, 'Deaths' column for 'LHRHa plus bicalutamide' is '274'."
  B: value="274" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 4 states: 'Analysis for OS occurred after the prespecified 525 deaths were observed (251 in the orteronel group and 274 in the control group).' Control group is the bicalutamide arm."

---
Column 12: PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment
Definition: What the hazard ratio with confidence intervals of progression-free survival (PFS) of all comparisons in all participants, regardless of metastasis type or volume, in the treatment arm of the trial? If reported, include the rate (percentage) and any accompanying details. If multiple arms are reported, list each arm separately. Use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="50.2% at 4 years" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="The 'Secondary End Points' section states 'The PFS at 4 years was 50.2% in the orteronel group'."
  B: value="50.2% at 4 years" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 4 states: 'The PFS at 4 years was 50.2% in the orteronel group and 33.9% in the control group (HR, 0.58; 95% CI, 0.51 to 0.67; P < .001).' Orteronel group is the treatment arm."

---
Column 13: PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Control
Definition: What hazard ratio with confidence intervals of Progression-Free Survival (PFS) of all comparisons in all participants in the control arm of the trial? If reported, include the rate (percentage) and any accompanying details. If multiple arms are reported, list each arm separately. Use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="33.9% at 4 years" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="The 'Secondary End Points' section states 'The PFS at 4 years was... 33.9% in the control group'."
  B: value="33.9% at 4 years" | page=4 | modality=text | reasoning="Page 4 states: 'The PFS at 4 years was 50.2% in the orteronel group and 33.9% in the control group (HR, 0.58; 95% CI, 0.51 to 0.67; P < .001).' Control group is the bicalutamide arm."

---
Column 14: TTPSA (mo) | Treatment
Definition: What time To Progression of Serum Androgen (in months) in the treatment arm of the clinical trial? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document defines PFS as including PSA progression but does not report a separate median time to PSA progression (TTPSA). It reports PSA response rates at 7 months but not a median time."
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="This specific metric was not found in the provided pages. Page 3 defines 'PSA response rates' but not 'Time To Progression of Serum Androgen (TTPSA)'."

---
Column 15: TTPSA (mo) | Control
Definition: What time to Progressive Serum Androgen (TTPSA) in months for participants in the control arm of the clinical trial? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document defines PFS as including PSA progression but does not report a separate median time to PSA progression (TTPSA). It reports PSA response rates at 7 months but not a median time."
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="This specific metric was not found in the provided pages. Page 3 defines 'PSA response rates' but not 'Time To Progression of Serum Androgen (TTPSA)'."


Submit verification for easy columns first. For disputed columns, use get_page then submit.

response.formatted_chunks:
[Page 1]
## Page 1

<::logo: Check for updates
A circular icon with a red bookmark on a yellow and blue background::>



original report
Orteronel for Metastatic
Hormone-Sensitive Prostate Cancer: A
Multicenter, Randomized, Open-Label Phase III
Trial (SWOG-1216)



Neeraj Agarwal, MD¹; Catherine M. Tangen, DrPH²; Maha H.A. Hussain, MD³; Shilpa Gupta, MD⁴; Melissa Plets, MS²; Primo N. Lara, MD⁵;
Andrea L. Harzstark, MD⁶; Przemyslaw W. Twardowski, MD⁷; Channing J. Paller, MD⁸; Dylan Zylla, MD⁹; Matthew R. Zibelman, MD¹⁰;
Ellis Levine, MD¹¹; Bruce J. Roth, MD¹²; Amir Goldkorn, MD¹³; Daniel A. Vaena, MD¹⁴,¹⁵; Manish Kohli, MD¹,¹⁶; Tony Crispino¹⁷;
Nicholas J. Vogelzang, MD¹⁸; Ian M. Thompson Jr, MD¹⁹; and David I. Quinn, MD¹³



abstr
PURPOSE Orteronel (TAK-700) is a nonsteroidal 17,20-lyase inhibitor suppressing androgen synthesis. We evaluated the clinical benefit of orteronel when added to androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) in patients with newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer.



act
METHODS In this open-label randomized phase III study, patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned 1:1 to ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily; experimental arm) or ADT with bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily; control arm). The primary objective was the comparison of overall survival (OS), targeting a 33% improvement in median survival. A stratified log-rank test with a one-sided P \u2264 .022 would indicate statistical significance. Secondary end points were progression-free survival (PFS), prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level at 7 months (\u2264 0.2 v 0.2 to \u2264 4 v > 4 ng/mL), and adverse event profile.



**RESULTS** Among 1,279 patients included in the analysis, 638 were randomly assigned to the ADT plus orteronel arm and 641 to the control arm. The median age was 68 years; 49% had extensive disease. After a median follow-up of 4.9 years, there was a significant improvement in PFS (median 47.6 v 23.0 months, hazard ratio 0.58; 95% CI, 0.51 to 0.67; P < .0001) and PSA response at 7 months (P < .0001), but not in OS (median 81.1 v 70.2 months, hazard ratio 0.86; 95% CI, 0.72 to 1.02; P = .040, one-sided). More grade 3/4 adverse events occurred in the experimental versus the control arms (43% v 14%). Postprotocol life-prolonging therapy was received by 77.4% of patients in the control arm and 61.3% of patients in the orteronel arm.



**CONCLUSION** The study did not meet the primary end point of improved OS with orteronel. The lack of correlation of PFS and PSA response with OS raises concerns over assumption of their consistent surrogacy for OS in the context of extensive postprotocol therapy in this setting.



J Clin Oncol 00. © 2022 by American Society of Clinical Oncology



## INTRODUCTION
The cornerstone of treatment for metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) is androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with medical or surgical castration.¹ However, disease progression universally occurs on ADT, mediated by extragonadal production of androgens or androgen-independent activation of androgen receptor.¹ The treatment paradigm of mHSPC has evolved over the past decade with multiple trials showing improved survival by intensification of ADT with either docetaxel chemotherapy or abiraterone, which inhibits a pivotal enzyme in intra- and extragonadal androgen synthesis (ie, CYP17 [CYP17 hydroxylase, CYP17, 20 lyase]) located in the testes and adrenal glands. ¹⁻⁵ More recently, two direct, potent, and specific inhibitors of the androgen receptor, apalutamide and



enzalutamide, were approved on the basis of improved survival in phase III trials. 1,6,7 Currently, the most common sequencing of these agents in the treatment of metastatic prostate cancer is abiraterone acetate followed by enzalutamide or docetaxel. 8 One challenge with abiraterone acetate is the requirement for coadministration of corticosteroids because of inhibition of CYP17 hydroxylase.1 This results in diminished cortisol production and leads to secondary mineralocorticoid excess, with symptoms such as electrolyte abnormalities, hypertension, and edema.1



Orteronel (TAK-700) is a novel CYP17 inhibitor. In
comparison with abiraterone acetate, orteronel inhibits
CYP17, 20 lyase with greater specificity compared with
CYP17 hydroxylase and does not generally lead to the
syndrome of secondary mineralocorticoid excess.9,10



ASCO®



Journal of Clinical Oncology®



1

S



ASSOCIATED
CONTENT
Appendix
**Data Sharing**
**Statement**
**Protocol**
Author affiliations
and support
information (if
applicable) appear
at the end of this
article.
Accepted on March
18, 2022 and
published at
ascopubs.org/journal/
jco on April 21, 2022:
DOI https://doi.org/10.
1200/JC0.21.02517

---

[Page 2]
## Page 2

Agarwal et al



CONTEXT

**Key Objective**
We examined if adding orteronel, a novel androgen axis inhibitor, to androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) would improve outcomes compared with ADT plus bicalutamide in men with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer in a phase III trial.

**Knowledge Generated**
Addition of orteronel to ADT resulted in a significant improvement in progression-free survival and prostate-specific antigen response but not in overall survival (OS), the primary end point. Interestingly, the median OS of patients in the control arm of SWOG-1216 was 24 months higher than that in patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer on the SWOG-9346 trial (reported in 2013) who had similar disease severity and also received ADT plus bicalutamide (70.2 v46 months).



## Relevance
These results indicate that orteronel is likely not an effective androgen axis inhibitor compared with recently approved agents in this setting. Higher than anticipated OS of patients in the control arm is reflective of therapeutic advancements made over the past decade in men with metastatic prostate cancer.



We hypothesized that the addition of orteronel to ADT would improve overall survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) response relative to bicalutamide with ADT among men with mHSPC.



## METHODS

### Trial Design and Conduct

The S1216 trial was a phase III, randomized, open-label, multicenter trial involving patients with mHSPC. The trial was designed by the SWOG study team and was approved by the National Cancer Institute (NCI). Patients were enrolled from 248 academic and community centers throughout the United States, incorporating three other NCI-funded cooperative groups (ECOG-ACRIN, ALLIANCE, and NRG). The NCI Central Institutional Review Board approved the initial version of the Protocol (online only) and all subsequent amendments. The study was conducted in accordance with the International Conference on Harmonization of Good Clinical Practice guidelines and the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. Signed written consent was obtained from all patients. First and last authors (N.A. and D.I.Q.) and the statisticians (C.M.T. and M.P.) among the authors assume responsibility for the completeness and accuracy of the data and analyses and for the fidelity of the trial to the Protocol.



## Patients and Interventions
Eligible patients were required to have histologically con-
firmed adenocarcinoma of the prostate and metastatic
disease as evidenced by soft tissue and/or bony metasta-
ses. Enrolled patients had a Zubrod performance status of
0-2; a Zubrod performance status of 3 was allowed if from
bone pain only and a PSA ≥ 2.0 ng/mL. No other prior
systemic therapy for metastatic prostate cancer was
allowed (with the exception of up to 30 days of ADT for



metastatic disease before registration), and at least 6 months
must have elapsed since completion of prior neoadjuvant
and/or adjuvant ADT. Concomitant radiotherapy was allowed
only for baseline symptoms per investigator's clinical judg-
ment during the first 4 months of protocol treatment. Patients
who had not started any therapy with luteinizing hormone-
releasing hormone (LHRH) agonist, antagonist, or orchi-
ectomy (early induction group) and patients who had already
started therapy with LHRH agonist, antagonist, or orchi-
ectomy within 30 days before registration (late induction
group) were eligible. Patients who were deemed to have
extensive mHSPC were eligible for enrollment if, on the basis
of the judgment of the treating physician, they were un-
suitable candidates for docetaxel or if they declined doce-
taxel therapy. 11



Exclusion criteria included brain metastases; prior therapy with ketoconazole, abiraterone acetate, or enzalutamide; New York Heart Association class III or IV heart failure at screening or thromboembolic event; unstable angina pectoris; myocardial infarction; or serious uncontrolled cardiac arrhythmia ≤ 6 months before registration.



Patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive
orteronel (300 mg) orally twice daily or bicalutamide (50
mg) administered orally once daily, in addition to contin-
uous ADT. Patient assignment was dynamically balanced
on three stratification factors: severity of disease (minimal v
extensive), Zubrod performance status (0-1 v 2-3), and
preregistration treatment status (early v late induction).
Severity (or risk) of disease criteria in this study was orig-
inally reported in 1989, and since then, it has been used in
all SWOG trials in the mHSPC setting to date. 11-13 Minimal
disease was defined as involvement of vertebrae and/or
pelvic bones and/or lymph nodes, and extensive disease
was defined as that with greater than minimal involvement.
Dynamic balancing is a general procedure for treatment



2 © 2022 by American Society of Clinical Oncology
